summary: Thread comparing 1-day vs 2-week lenses; users split on whether Acuvue or B&L was drier — some switched away from Acuvue to B&L for relief, others switched back. No clean brand winner.
User reported Acuvue Moist left her eyes dry to the point of falling out; switched to CooperVision and found it both cheaper and better-fitting.
One Day Acuvue described as comfortable to wear but very hard to insert because the lens is so thin it keeps folding on itself.
Long-time wearer started getting conjunctivitis and red eyes after years on Acuvue 2-week lenses; replies suggested switching to B&L or moving to dailies for hygiene. Health concern raised.
Poster summarised their own verdict after trying multiple brands: positive for Acuvue, negative for CooperVision.

Long-running price-comparison thread: One Day Acuvue reported at HK$155–180 per 30-pack at small independent shops vs HK$220–290 at Optical 88; users directed each other to online sellers for lower prices. Mild frustration at chain markup.
Users compared per-box Acuvue Moist pricing across shops ranging from HK$130 (no astigmatism) to HK$530 (with astigmatism, two boxes); mild frustration at astigmatism premium.
First-time wearer spent about an hour getting the lens in and had even worse trouble removing it; the shop's own tutorial videos did not help. Onboarding friction complaint.

Discussion concluded removal difficulty with Acuvue One Day was about base-curve mismatch (8.5mm) rather than the brand itself; users self-corrected an initial brand complaint into a general fit issue.

Dailies Total 1 described as more comfortable and less drying than B&L by multiple users; caveat is it is harder to remove than other dailies. One optician praised for coaching a nervous first-time wearer.
Thread recommends Alcon Naphcon-A allergy relief drops; user noted skipping the ophthalmologist and asking the pharmacy directly to save money.
Alcon antibiotic eye drops prescribed for infection; user recommends asking pharmacist directly for the Alcon product to avoid doctor visit cost.
B&L colored daily lenses comfortable enough for all-day wear; one user noted they cling tightly and are hard to remove.
Within-brand packaging distinction: user found the blue/white box SofLens too soft and prone to tearing, preferring the firmer green/white box version. Not a brand-level verdict.
B&L described as easy to insert but uncomfortable with eyes drying out quickly — inverse trade-off to Acuvue in the same thread.
Opinion roughly 50/50 split: some users found B&L more comfortable after switching from Acuvue; others found the reverse.
User described fully switching from J&J Acuvue to CooperVision EV and Proclear, finding them more comfortable and cheaper than Acuvue Moist and TruEye.
Mixed: one user wore CooperVision toric for years until a bad batch had the wrong prescription; another found CooperVision dried her eyes within three days and returned to her optician.
Explicit comparative verdict: CooperVision more comfortable and cheaper than Acuvue Moist.
User named CooperVision 1-day as good for astigmatism, non-drying, and high oxygen permeability; second user agreed.
CooperVision praised as firmer and less prone to folding during insertion compared to Acuvue's thinner lens.
User asked where to buy lenses and listed Olens as an option; reply steered toward HKTVmall on price. Olens on local radar but loses on marketplace pricing for price-sensitive buyers.
Technical sub-thread comparing Olens water content (38%/48% depending on line) against Acuvue Moist/Dailies/TruEye. Purely informational, no comfort verdict reached.

First-time wearer reviewed Acuvue Oasys with Hydraclear Plus as noticeably more comfortable than B&L and Duna lenses she had tried before; no dryness even through a full day including a long bus ride.
In an Acuvue vs B&L comparison thread, respondent found Acuvue most comfortable overall after trying Freshlook, Acuvue, and Bloom Alice, though it slightly obscured her pupil visually.

Beauty blogger first-person account of attending a J&J launch event for 1-Day Acuvue Define; enthusiastic but brand-event coverage rather than independent purchase review — should be weighted lightly.

Repeated standalone questions asking about Acuvue monthly-lens pricing at Top Charoen optical chain, including for high prescriptions up to -1200; suggests Thai shoppers treat Acuvue as a premium price point worth researching carefully.
Dry-eye sufferer laid out side-by-side specs (oxygen transmission, water content) for Acuvue Oasys vs Alcon Air Optix Aqua to decide which to try. Purely comparative, no verdict reached in-thread.

User on B&L Soflens 59 said a shop recommended bordering on insisted she switch to Alcon O2 Optix with a string of claimed benefits; her tone reads as skeptical of the sales pitch rather than of the Alcon product itself.
Recurring comparisons of Alcon Systane Ultra and Renu solution against competitors are consistently neutral to positive; treated as a reliable staple rather than a source of complaints.

User reported sudden strong eye redness and stinging on the very first day wearing B&L Ultra monthly lenses, despite no previous issues on B&L Biotrue dailies. Unresolved safety concern specific to the Ultra line.
New wearer reported B&L Ultra lenses sticking to fingers and being difficult to insert in her first few days. Mildly negative, framed as a learning-curve issue.
Several new or returning wearers describe B&L SofLens 59 monthly lenses as comfortable — not too hard or soft — with no irritation reported in early days of wear.
Independent consumer review of B&L Naturelle daily color lens is enthusiastic about natural-looking color effect and overall comfort.
Heavy daily wearer stayed loyal to B&L monthly lenses for comfort and was anxious about switching to the cheaper local brand Duna, reflecting comfort-driven loyalty rather than price sensitivity.

Long-time Korean color-lens user described Olens as far more comfortable than Thai big-eye lenses she had tried — feels like not wearing anything. Referenced Blackpink endorsement as part of her interest in the brand.

Beauty blogger reviewed several Olens color-lens designs (Someday Gray, Winky Stay Brown, Triple 3 Con Gray); generally positive about natural-looking colors and comfort, flagging which shades look too obviously colored for school wear.
Loyal O-Lens user compared two of the brand's own lines and found Vivi Ring noticeably less satisfying than her usual Jenith Gold — a within-brand disappointment rather than a brand-level rejection.

Multiple threads ask where to buy Olens or for alternatives with a similarly natural look and comparable water content, suggesting real brand pull from Blackpink and NewJeans endorsements but frustration with limited physical retail availability in Thailand.

User questions authenticity of unusually cheap Olens and Idollens lenses sold through pre-order resale groups at THB 350-380 versus THB 500-990 through other channels; worried about counterfeits despite sellers' assurances.
